data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0848 ET - One of the more notable items in a rather ho-hum Canada jobs report for February is a sharp drop in hours worked. Hours worked decline 1.3%, or the largest month-over-month decline in nearly three years. That's in contrast to the 0.9% jump in January. Hours worked is a decent gauge for economic activity and a rough preview for monthly GDP data. The data agency attributes the sharp drop to "notable winter storms" during the week the labor-force survey was conducted, which in this case was Feb. 9 to Feb. 15. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0843 ET - The euro's recent appreciation might not last given the threat of U.S. tariffs, Nordea analyst Philip Maldia Madsen says in a note. The euro's gains reflect the prospect of increased eurozone fiscal spending boosting the economy, he says. It's also supported by a weaker dollar as President Trump's tariffs and plans to reduce government spending dampen the economic outlook, he says. However, it's only a matter of time before Trump shifts his focus for tariffs over to Europe. "After the recent rally in the euro above $1.08, we believe the upside is limited and that a setback is likely in the coming months." The euro hits a four-month high of $1.0890, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 ET - Treasury yields and the dollar fall as U.S. jobs creation accelerates less than expected amid a decline in government employment. February payroll were 151,000, up from January's downward revised 125,000. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expected 170,000. Unemployment rises to 4.1% from 4%. Employment in the federal government declines by 10,000. Both Treasury yields and the dollar were already weakening ahead of the data and didn't change course. The 10-year is at 4.246% and the two-year at 3.932%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.4%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0819 ET - The February jobs report comes amid a gloomier labor-market landscape, Renaissance Macro's Neil Dutta writes. Granted, initial-jobless claims fell in the week through March 1 and reversed some of the previous week's jump, but continuing claims, which track the size of the unemployed population, are back near January's three-year high. "Those out of work are facing increasing difficulty securing new employment," Dutta writes. Meanwhile, the Challenger report indicates a surge of announced layoff plans in February, the highest level since July 2020, Dutta notes. "This surge in announced layoffs, coupled with the rise in continuing claims, reinforces concerns about the labor market's health," writes Dutta.(matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

0756 ET - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0756 ET - This week's 25 basis-point interest-rate cut by the European Central Bank is likely to support corporate bond refinancing, LBBW credit analysts say in a note. The ECB on Thursday lowered the key eurozone rate to 2.50%. "This is good news for refinancing conditions and could partially [neutralize] the effect of the short-term rise in yields," LBBW says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0735 ET - The extent of the dollar's recent depreciation means the bar is higher for a significant negative reaction to potentially weaker U.S. nonfarm payrolls data at 1330 GMT, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. Weaker data would "certainly reinforce fears that the U.S. economy could be headed for a downturn" and that the economy is in "no position to weather the wave of tariffs expected in April." However, it might take much weaker data for the dollar to fall materially further, he says. If investors see increased risks of a deeper economic downturn, the euro could rise closer to $1.1000, he says. The euro rises 0.6% to $1.0847, having earlier reached a four-month high of $1.0871, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 ET - Yields on euro-denominated junk bonds are less attractive relative to their investment-grade peers as yields on investment-grade bonds have advanced at a faster pace, Barclays analysts say in a note. "From the perspective of yield-driven demand, there is an argument to be made that [euro high-yield] is looking less compelling." The yield differential between riskier euro high-yield bonds and lower-risk euro investment-grade bonds has compressed by 186 basis points, the lowest since 2019, making euro junk bonds less appealing, Barclays says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0629 ET - The dollar's weakness comes as the Trump administration's highly uncertain tariff policy appears to be damaging investor confidence and economic activity, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. U.K. investment slumped on uncertainty leading up to Brexit and "the same can be true of the U.S. where businesses and consumers just want some certainty and are not getting it so far." The dollar has also been hit by softer U.S. data and markets pricing in more Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. The dollar could fall further if U.S. nonfarm payrolls data at 1330 GMT are softer, he says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 103.752 after hitting a four-month low of 103.563 earlier. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0617 ET - AllianceBernstein expects the European Central Bank to stop interest-rate cuts at a higher level than it previously expected, European economist Sandra Rhouma says in a note. The adjustment comes against the backdrop of growth risks being less tilted toward the downside, more expansionary fiscal policy and on assumption that the European Central Bank nears neutral policy, she says. "Consequently, my revised baseline for the ECB's policy rate at the end of 2025 is 2%, up from the previous 1.75%," she says. "The case for the ECB cutting below 2% by year-end appears weaker, given the medium-term risks." The ECB will likely opt to hold at 2% if their projections indicate higher inflation in the coming years, rather than raising rates in 2026. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0612 ET - The cost of insuring euro credit against default using credit default swaps rises in cautious trading ahead of U.S. jobs data due to be released at 1330 GMT. Markets expect a weak reading on the jobs data due to the recent layoffs in U.S. government agencies, Swissquote Bank senior analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. A lower-than-forecast jobs number and higher-than-expected wage growth could be a "worse-case-scenario for markets," she says. "[This] combination would leave the Fed facing a slowing economy with limited room to give support." The iTraxx Europe Crossover index which tracks euro junk bond CDS, rises 4 basis points to 295bps, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0607 ET - Vanguard now expects just one more interest-rate cut from the European Central Bank this year, having previously expected three, says senior economist Shaan Raithatha in a note. The change comes on the back of Germany's large-scale infrastructure and defense program, greater EU-wide defense spending and the prospect of a ceasefire in Ukraine. "We take out two cuts from our previous forecast," he says. "We now expect the ECB policy rate to settle at 2.25%, likely reached in June or September." The ECB cut rates by 25 basis points this week, lowering the deposit rate to 2.50%. Markets currently price in about 40 basis points of cuts this year, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: FORRESTER RESEARCH, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Forrester Research, Inc., a global independent research and advisory firm, has released its 2024 10-K report, detailing the company's financial and operational performance over the past year. The report highlights key financial metrics, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks faced by the company. Forrester's focus on empowering leaders in technology, customer experience, digital, marketing, sales, and product functions remains central to its business model, with an emphasis on customer obsession as a driver for growth.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $432.5 million, a decrease of 10% compared to 2023 due to declines in research, consulting, and events revenues.
  • Income from Operations: $0.7 million, a significant decrease from $6.8 million in 2023, reflecting lower revenues and restructuring costs.
  • Net Income (Loss): $(5.7) million, compared to a net income of $3.1 million in 2023, impacted by a loss from the sale of a divested operation and higher income tax expense.
  • Basic Income (Loss) Per Common Share: $(0.30), compared to $0.16 in 2023, reflecting the net loss for the year.
  • Diluted Income (Loss) Per Common Share: $(0.30), compared to $0.16 in 2023, consistent with the basic loss per share due to the net loss.

Business Highlights

  • Business Model and Strategy: Forrester operates as a global independent research and advisory firm, focusing on empowering leaders in technology, customer experience, digital, marketing, sales, and product functions. The company emphasizes customer obsession as a key driver for growth, offering proprietary research, consulting, and events to help clients achieve their goals.
  • Revenue Segments: Forrester's business is divided into three segments: Research, Consulting, and Events. Research revenues, which include subscription-based products like Forrester Decisions, make up the majority of the company's revenue. Consulting revenues are derived from project-based engagements and advisory services, while Events revenues come from ticket and sponsorship sales for Forrester-hosted events.
  • Geographical Performance: The company delivers its products and services globally, with a significant presence in North America, Europe, and the Asia-Pacific region. Revenues from customers outside the U.S. accounted for 23% of total revenues in 2024, reflecting a slight increase from the previous year.
  • Sales and Marketing: Forrester employs a direct sales force organized by client size, geography, and market potential. The sales organization includes Premier groups for large clients and Emerging and Mid-Size Tech groups for smaller clients. The company also utilizes independent sales representatives in select international locations.
  • Client Base: As of December 31, 2024, Forrester served over 1,900 client companies, with no single client accounting for more than 3% of total revenues. The company focuses on maintaining high client retention and wallet retention rates, which are critical metrics for its subscription-based business model.
  • Operational Changes: In 2024, Forrester implemented workforce reductions and office closures to align its cost structure with revenue outlooks. These actions were part of a broader strategy to streamline operations and support future contract value growth.
  • Future Outlook: Forrester plans to continue investing in its go-to-market structure and creating products that drive contract value growth. The company anticipates that its focus on customer obsession and the transition to the Forrester Decisions product platform will support long-term growth and profitability.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Divestiture: The company divested its non-core FeedbackNow product line in August 2024 for $17.6 million, receiving $6.0 million in cash, a $9.0 million note receivable, and a $2.6 million non-marketable equity investment. This move aligns with the company's strategy to focus on its core research and advisory services.
  • Workforce Reductions: The company implemented workforce reductions in February 2024 and January 2025 to better align its cost structure with revenue outlooks, reflecting a strategic initiative to streamline operations and improve efficiency.
  • Capital Management: The company repurchased $15.9 million of its common stock in 2024, with a remaining authorization of approximately $80.0 million for future repurchases. The company also maintained a credit facility with $150.0 million of revolving credit commitments, of which $35.0 million was outstanding as of December 31, 2024. The facility allows for an increase in commitments up to $50.0 million, subject to conditions. The company used $16.1 million of cash in financing activities in 2024, primarily for stock repurchases and taxes related to stock-based compensation.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its focus on core research and advisory services, leveraging its Forrester Decisions product platform, which constituted 80% of its contract value by the end of 2024. The company expects to remain in compliance with its credit facility covenants and anticipates that its current cash balance and cash flows from operations will meet its working capital and capital expenditure requirements for the next twelve months. Additionally, the company is monitoring its stock price and may perform a quantitative impairment test if necessary, which could impact future financial results.

Challenges and Risks

  • Subscription-Based Research Services: A decline in renewals or demand for subscription-based research services could adversely affect the company's financial performance. Retaining existing subscriptions and increasing contract value is crucial for success.
  • Consulting Services Demand: Consulting revenues, which are project-based and non-recurring, have decreased. A failure to generate new consulting engagements could negatively impact financial results.
  • Economic Environment: The business is sensitive to economic conditions such as inflation, rising interest rates, and geopolitical tensions, which could reduce technology spending and demand for services.
  • International Operations: Operating in 73 countries exposes the company to risks such as political instability, regulatory changes, and currency fluctuations, which could impact results.
  • Generative AI: The introduction of generative AI tools like Izola presents risks including potential factual errors, data privacy issues, and intellectual property challenges.
  • Key Management and Staff Retention: The loss of key management, particularly the CEO, and challenges in attracting and retaining qualified staff could hinder business growth.
  • Market Trends: The ability to anticipate and respond to rapidly changing market trends is critical. Failure to do so could affect the company's competitive position.
  • Government Contracting Risks: Business with the U.S. government is subject to risks such as contract termination and compliance with regulations, which could lead to revenue loss.
  • Outstanding Debt: The company's debt obligations could restrict business operations and financial flexibility, impacting the ability to respond to competitive pressures.
  • Competition: The company faces intense competition from both direct and indirect competitors, which could lead to pricing pressure and loss of market share.
  • Network Disruptions and Security Breaches: Potential security breaches could damage the company's reputation and operational results.
  • Intellectual Property Protection: Challenges in protecting intellectual property, especially in international markets, could adversely affect the business.
  • Privacy and Regulatory Compliance: Compliance with evolving privacy laws and regulations, such as GDPR and CCPA, could incur substantial costs and impact operations.
  • Taxation Risks: Changes in tax laws or increased taxation in certain jurisdictions could negatively affect financial results.

SEC Filing: FORRESTER RESEARCH, INC. [ FORR ] - 10-K - Mar. 07, 2025

data.title: Yuexiu Property Logs 6.23 Billion Yuan in February Contracted Sales
data.description:
Yuexiu Property recorded contracted sales of around 6.23 billion yuan in February, up 63.2% from the year-ago period, while the gross floor area decreased by 20% to roughly 115,700 square meters, according to a Friday disclosure with the Hong Kong Exchange.

For the two months ended February, contracted sales amounted to 12.8 billion yuan, 23.8% higher year-on-year, while the corresponding gross floor area decreased by 6.7% to 348,400 square meters.

Through a union of its unit with others, the company acquired an effective equity interest of around 48.45% in a land parcel in Shanghai by way of open tender.

The said land parcel has a gross floor area of 104,369 square meters, is located in China's Yangsi sector, Pudong District in Shanghai, and is planned for residential use. The land premium attributable to the company's effective equity interest in the land parcel amounts to 2.47 billion yuan.

Shares closed over 1% higher on Friday.

data.title: CIBC Comments on Canada's Labor Report for February
data.description:
Hiring stalled during February, in what could be the first sign that United States tariff uncertainty is impacting the Canadian economy, said CIBC after the release of Friday's Labour Force Suvey (LFS).

The 1,100 gain was below the 20,000 expected by the consensus, and the unemployment rate only held steady at 6.6% due to a drop in labor force participation, noted the bank.

The sector breakdown showed a strong gain in wholesale & retail employment, offset by declines in other areas, including some that are most exposed to tariff uncertainty, such as manufacturing and transportation & wholesaling, stated CIBC.

Hours worked fell 1.3% on the month, although that reflected large winter storms that hit central and eastern Canada during the reference period. Hourly earnings for permanent employees accelerated to 4.0% from 3.7%, but the decline in hours worked due to winter storms could have caused some volatility here.

Given the stall in hiring during February and ongoing uncertainty regarding tariffs that are likely to have larger negative impacts ahead, CIBC continues to expect a 25bps cut from the Bank of Canada next Wednesday.

data.title: Jobs Report Seen as Supporting Fed Cut in June — Market Talk
data.description: February payrolls may change the Fed's calculations for interest rates this year, GDS Wealth Management's Glen Smith writes. Job creation was smaller than expected and unemployment ticked up to 4.1% from 4%. Federal government employment declines by 10,000, but Smith notes that the report still doesn't account for most job cuts from DOGE. He says it is possible that a Fed cut will come in June. Chair Powell is speaking at 12:30p.m. ET. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Canada Jobs Rise Much Less Than Expected in February; Unemployment Rate Unchanged at 6.6%
data.description:
Canadian employment was virtually unchanged in February, rising by 1,100, or flat percentage-wise, month-over-month as the employment rate held steady at 61.1% and the unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%, said the country's statistical agency on Friday.

February's job gains were much lower than the 19,700 consensus rise provided by MUFG. The unemployment rate was better than the 6.7% consensus rate also provided by the bank.

In February, employment rose among core-aged — 25 to 54 years old — women, up 27,000 or 0.4% month-over-month, while it fell among women aged 55 years and older, down 15,000 or 0.8%, noted Statistics Canada in its Labour Force Survey (LFS).

For core-aged men, employment was little changed in February, following three consecutive months of increases totalling 90,000 (+1.3%) in November, December and January.

Employment increased in wholesale and retail trade (+51, 000; +1.7% month over month) as well as finance, insurance, real estate, rental and leasing (+16, 000; +1.1%). There were declines in professional, scientific and technical services (-33, 000; -1.6%) and transportation and warehousing (-23, 000; -2.1%).

Total hours worked fell 1.3% in the month but were up 0.5% compared with 12 months earlier. Notable winter storms buried parts of Central and Eastern Canada in snow throughout the LFS reference week of Feb. 9 to 15, stated the Ottawa-based agency.

Average hourly wages among employees were up 3.8% (+$1.32 to $36.14) on a year-over-year basis in February, following a growth of 3.5% in January — not seasonally adjusted, added StatsCan.

data.title: Philip Morris International Affiliate Settles Tobacco Claims in Canada for $22.7 Billion
data.description:
Philip Morris International said Friday that its de-consolidated Canadian affiliate, Rothmans, Benson & Hedges, has had its plan of compromise and arrangement approved by a Canadian court.

The company said the plan resolves all tobacco-related claims and litigation in Canada against Rothmans, Benson & Hedges, and its affiliates, including Philip Morris International.

The resolution, also approved for Imperial Tobacco Canada and JTI-Macdonald, involves a global settlement of 32.5 billion Canadian dollars ($22.7 billion), the company said.

Philip Morris International said the settlement will be funded through an upfront payment based on available cash and cash equivalents, plus annual payments based on the companies' net after-tax income.

Rothmans, Benson & Hedges will retain $CA750 million for its discretion and will be released from claims related to its combustible and smokeless tobacco products prior to the effective date of the plan, it added.

data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Steady at 6.6% in February
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Unemployment in Canada held steady in February as the size of the labor force shrank and hiring flattened.

Statistics Canada reported Friday that Canada's economy added a net 1,100 jobs last month. Market expectations were for employment to increase by 20,000, after even more aggressive hiring the previous three months, according to economists at TD Securities.

The unemployment rate was unchanged at 6.6%. Expectations were for the jobless rate to rise to 6.7%.

When calculated using U.S. Labor Department methodology, Canada's unemployment rate in February was unmoved at 5.6%.

Write to Robb M. Stewart robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Broadcom, Nvidia, HPE, Gap, Walgreens, Costco, Intuitive Machines, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel

Stock futures were rising Friday ahead of the release of the monthly U.S. jobs report following a Wall Street selloff that had investors rattled by frequent shifts in trade policy from the Trump administration. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite finished Thursday in correction territory.

These stocks were poised to make moves Friday:

Broadcom rose 9.3% after the semiconductor and software posted better-than-expected fiscal first-quarter earnings and issued strong guidance. Broadcom reported adjusted earnings in the first quarter of $1.60 a share, higher than analysts' expectations of $1.51. Revenue of $14.92 topped consensus of $14.62 billion. Artificial-intelligence revenue in the quarter was $4.1 billion, up 77% from a year earlier. For the current second quarter, Broadcom anticipates revenue of $14.9 billion compared with expectations of $14.71 billion. CEO Hock Tan said Broadcom's largest technology customers continue to "invest aggressively" in their next generation AI models.

Nvidia, the leading maker of AI chips, was up 0.9% in premarket trading after tumbling 5.7% on Thursday. Broadcom's report could inject some life into the faltering AI trade. Nvidia shares have declined 18% this year.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise sank 18% after fiscal first-quarter earnings and second-quarter guidance missed Wall Street expectations and the server and cloud-software company said it would begin a cost-cutting program that will reduce its overall headcount by about 5%, or about 2,500 jobs. HPE said it expects second-quarter adjusted earnings of 28 cents to 34 cents a share on revenue of $7.2 billion to $7.6 billion. Analysts expect adjusted earnings of 50 cents on revenue of $7.92 billion. As for the tariffs situation, CEO Antonio Neri told Barron's that it was a "very fluid, complicated situation, with a lot of moving parts and twists." The CEO said HPE does a lot of manufacturing in Mexico.

Marvell Technology rose 0.2% in premarket trading after the chip maker sank nearly 20% on Thursday, the stock's largest percentage decrease since Sept. 27, 2001, when it fell 32%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. On Wednesday, Marvell issued fiscal first-quarter guidance that was mostly in line with expectations, disappointing investors who had thought the company might offer even more positive forecasts.

Gap was jumping 16% after the apparel retailer topped fourth-quarter earnings expectations and delivered stronger-than-expected profit guidance for the current fiscal year. Gap sees total company sales in the fiscal year rising between 1% to 2% versus Wall Street expectations of a 1.7% increase to $15.7 billion. The retailer projects an increase in operating income of between 8% and 10% from $1.1 billion in 2024.

Walgreens Boots Alliance will be taken private by Sycamore Partners in a $23.7 billion deal. Sycamore will pay $11.45 a share in cash, representing an equity value of around $10 billion. Shareholders also will receive up to $3 in cash per share representing the future sale of the company's debt and equity interests in VillageMD. The companies expect the deal to close in the fourth quarter. Walgreens rose 6.6% to $11.30 in premarket trading.

Fiscal second-quarter earnings at Costco Wholesale, the warehouse retailer, missed analysts' estimates, sending shares down 1.9% in premarket trading. Costco reported earnings of $4.02 a share, below estimates of $4.09, as revenue of $63.7 billion was slightly better than projections of $63.1 billion. Same-store sales rose 6.8% and beat estimates that called for a 6.4% increase.

Intuitive Machines was tumbling 36% after difficulties landing the company's Athena spaceship near the moon's south pole on Thursday. The craft touched down around noon Eastern time, but NASA's broadcast of the landing finished without clear information on the status of the craft.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

data.title: Oil Futures Gain Ground as U.S. Tariffs Get Pared Back For a Month — Market Talk
data.description:
0845 ET - Oil futures attempt a recovery after President Trump postpones import tariffs on a range of goods from Mexico and Canada for another month, easing one of the bearish factors behind the recent selloff that include OPEC+ plans to start unwinding cuts next month. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Rystad Energy's Mukesh Sahdev says in a note. "The tariff threats sent shockwaves through markets, not just because of potential trade disruptions, but also due to fears of retaliatory moves that could ripple through energy supply chains." WTI is up 1.5% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is up 1.5% at $70.52 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0840 ET - Treasury yields and the dollar fall as U.S. jobs creation accelerates less than expected amid a decline in government employment. February payroll were 151,000, up from January's downward revised 125,000. Economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal expected 170,000. Unemployment rises to 4.1% from 4%. Employment in the federal government declines by 10,000. Both Treasury yields and the dollar were already weakening ahead of the data and didn't change course. The 10-year is at 4.246% and the two-year at 3.932%. The WSJ Dollar Index falls 0.4%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0819 ET - The February jobs report comes amid a gloomier labor-market landscape, Renaissance Macro's Neil Dutta writes. Granted, initial-jobless claims fell in the week through March 1 and reversed some of the previous week's jump, but continuing claims, which track the size of the unemployed population, are back near January's three-year high. "Those out of work are facing increasing difficulty securing new employment," Dutta writes. Meanwhile, the Challenger report indicates a surge of announced layoff plans in February, the highest level since July 2020, Dutta notes. "This surge in announced layoffs, coupled with the rise in continuing claims, reinforces concerns about the labor market's health," writes Dutta.(matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

0756 ET - Canada's auto industry likely won't face a prolonged tariff situation, according to TD Cowen. In a report, analyst Brian Morrison says that the recent implementation then reversal of tariffs shows the complexity of the auto supply chain and how prolonged tariff duration would be negative to the U.S.'s own auto industry and economy. "This is not the intent of the Trump administration, in our view, and as such we anticipate in time to see modest changes favouring a gradual shift to U.S. content with limited disruption by H2/25," he says. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0716 ET - Cleveland-Cliffs is launching a company-wide "Buy American" incentive for its nearly 30,000 employees. During calendar year 2025, any Cleveland-Cliffs employee who buys or leases a new American-built vehicle with substantial Cliffs' steel content will receive a $1,000 cash bonus in connection with the purchase. Vehicles produced in a plant outside of the U.S., or that use a significant amount of imported flat-rolled steel, will not qualify for the incentive. "We hope that other companies will follow suit and do their part to incentivize consumption of American-made products," says Lourenco Goncalves, Cliffs' chairman and CEO. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0549 ET - French artificial-intelligence startup Mistral AI is open to teaming up with telecommunication companies in the U.S., Chief Executive Officer Arthur Mensch said in an interview at the Mobile World Congress in Barcelona. "That would be on the table," he said, noting that some discussions were taking place. Mistral AI is already partnering with telecommunication companies in Europe, including Orange and Free, integrating Mistral AI's applications into some of their products and using AI to improve network performance. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0845 ET - Oil futures attempt a recovery after President Trump postpones import tariffs on a range of goods from Mexico and Canada for another month, easing one of the bearish factors behind the recent selloff that include OPEC+ plans to start unwinding cuts next month. "While the delay in tariffs has provided a brief sigh of relief, the market is still walking a tightrope between policy uncertainty and oversupply concerns," Rystad Energy's Mukesh Sahdev says in a note. "The tariff threats sent shockwaves through markets, not just because of potential trade disruptions, but also due to fears of retaliatory moves that could ripple through energy supply chains." WTI is up 1.5% at $67.34 a barrel, and Brent is up 1.5% at $70.52 a barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0827 ET - Oil's recent selloff might have been overdone in light of current market dynamics, according to SEB's chief commodities analyst, Bjarne Schieldrop. Brent crude and WTI both regained some ground on Friday, rising 1.4% higher to $70.46 and $67.30 a barrel, respectively. "The recent selloff was a little too much and a little too soon as [a] surplus is not here quite yet," Schieldrop says. Meanwhile, the price difference between Brent contracts for one month and three months ahead is widening. "The Brent one-month price is currently trading very soft versus the one-three month timespreads," according to the analyst. "So, more rebound is definitely possible given both time-spreads and technically oversold market." Still, crude futures are still on track for weekly losses of over 3% amid concerns over U.S. tariffs and OPEC+'s decision to start raising supply in April. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0438 ET - European natural-gas prices regain some ground after tumbling in the previous trading session, with the benchmark Dutch TTF up 3.4% to 39.60 euros a megawatt hour. Prices opened at 37.72 euros--their lowest level since September--and are still headed for a weekly loss of more than 10%. U.S. President Trump is pushing for a quick end to the war in Ukraine, raising prospects that Russian gas flows could resume soon, while EU officials delayed the release of a proposal to end the bloc's reliance on Russian fossil fuel imports. Plus, the European Commission recommended adding more flexibility to storage filling targets, relieving some pressure off the market amid growing concerns over fast depleting inventories. According to Gas Infrastructure Europe, EU storage is currently 37.2% full, well below the five-year average. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0355 ET - Oil prices rise in early trade but remain on track for a significant weekly loss amid fluctuating U.S. trade policies and OPEC+ output hike plans. Brent crude and WTI are both up 1.2% to $70.29 and $67.21 a barrel, respectively, after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the U.S. will intensify pressure on Iran and won't hesitate to go "all in" on sanctions on Russia if it helps in peace negotiations. Still, "oil markets are dealing with renewed uncertainty on the supply side and a darkening outlook on the demand side," analysts at Fitch's research unit BMI say. The U.S. partially pulled back tariffs on some goods from Mexico and Canada, adding further uncertainty over President Trump's trade strategy and its impact on global economic growth. Meanwhile, news that additional OPEC+ supply will soon hit the market is increasing downward pressure on prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0303 ET - A revival in demand for goods produced at German factories lingers on the horizon, Commerzbank economist Ralph Solveen says. Factory orders slumped at the start of the year, figures show Friday, quashing much of the optimism generated with the previous month's surge in orders. Orders seem to be stabilising, but a sustained rebound in demand is a long way off yet, Solveen says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0131 ET - Vale Indonesia's new nickel-ore sales initiatives are expected to be a key catalyst in 2025, Nomura's Ahmad Maghfur Usman and Verdhana Sekuritas analysts say in a research report. The nickel miner has disclosed improved feasibility for its upstream mine and high-pressure acid leach projects, with a planned gross investment of US$8.6 billion, the analysts note. Ore sales could drive a three-year revenue CAGR of 29%, according to the brokerage's estimates. The company's limonite ore will supply four new HPAL facilities set to come online by late 2026. Nomura upgrades the stock's rating to buy from neutral, maintaining a target price of IDR4,000.00. Shares are 10.6% higher at IDR3,140.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Employers Added 151,000 Jobs in February, Unemployment Rose to 4.1% — WSJ
data.description:
By Justin Lahart

The U.S. continued to generate jobs at a steady pace in February, offering reassurances that the labor market has remained relatively stable since President Trump took office.

The U.S. added a seasonally adjusted 151,000 jobs in February, the Labor Department reported Friday, slightly below the gain of 170,000 jobs economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected to see.

That was better than the 125,000 jobs added in January.

The unemployment rate, which is based on a separate survey from the jobs figures, rose to 4.1% from 4%.

Economists believe that the cuts to federal government jobs that have come since President Trump came into office came too late to be captured in the February figures. But the report did show a loss of 10,000 federal government jobs, while government jobs at the state and local level increased by 21,000.

Average hourly earnings rose 0.3% from January, putting them 4% above their year-earlier level.

Economists expect that the combination of government job layoffs, reduced government funding, uncertainty over tariffs and immigration restrictions will at least temporarily weigh on employment growth in the months ahead.

Stock futures rose after the report was released.

For its jobs figure, the Labor Department surveys employers on how many people they had on their payrolls during the pay period that includes the 12th of the month; anybody who worked at all during that period in February was counted as employed. That includes federal workers still in their probationary period after getting hired or promoted that the White House began laying off around Feb. 13. It also includes the roughly 75,000 people who accepted the White House's "deferred resignation" offer, where the White House said they don't have to work but can still get paid through the end of September.

Those layoffs will begin showing up in the jobs count in the months ahead. Exactly how many federal government workers have lost their jobs so far as a result of the "Workforce Optimization Initiative" led by Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency is unclear. Some firings might not have been announced, while in other cases fired workers have been called back to the office. There are also layoffs at private-sector employers where government contracts and grants have been canceled.

Outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas on Thursday said that it counted 63,583 layoff announcements in February in the federal workforce and in government contractors. Labor Department data also show that there has been a pickup in initial claims for unemployment benefits filed by federal government employees.

Beyond any layoffs, the government could also lose a substantial number of jobs due to attrition. President Trump put a hiring freeze in place on Jan. 20, the day he was inaugurated.

Strategists at Evercore ISI worry that uncertainty over contracts and grants is having "an additional paralyzing effect on hiring at employers exposed to these funding sources or in need of regulatory approvals from agencies that are themselves paralyzed by DOGE review."

Many businesses are enthusiastic about Trump's plans to cut taxes and scale back regulations, and that could lead them to increase hiring. The White House believes that tariffs will encourage automakers and other manufacturers to bring more production, and jobs, to the U.S., and that cuts to government jobs and spending will ultimately make the economy more efficient.

Belt-tightening by government workers who have lost their jobs, or fear losing them, has begun to show up. Card spending by Bank of America customers in the Washington, D.C., metropolitan area has slowed, according to an analysis conducted by the bank's economists.

Worries over tariffs could also weigh on the labor market. This is in part because uncertainty over what imports from what countries will ultimately be subject to higher tariffs, and at what rates, makes it hard for businesses to hire. In the Federal Reserve's compilation of economic anecdotes, known as the beige book, released Wednesday, the Dallas Fed noted that "tariffs and the impacts of shifting trade policies on prices were a source of significant concern for businesses across many industries."

Immigration policy could also restrain the job market. The number of people coming into the U.S. began declining last summer, but since Inauguration Day appears to have dropped sharply. The Department of Homeland Security said in a Feb. 20 release that "daily border encounters" had plunged 93% since Trump took office. Additionally, worries about deportations could lead some people to withdraw from the labor force. Ahead of Friday's release, JPMorgan Chase economists assumed that slowing immigration would cut into February's employment count by 25,000 jobs.

Write to Justin Lahart at Justin.Lahart@wsj.com

data.title: Wall St turns to jobs picture
data.description:
Morning Bid U.S.

What matters in U.S. and global markets today

By Mike Dolan, Editor-At-Large, Financial Industry and Financial Markets

In my Friday newsletters, I'll now offer you an overview of the day's market-moving news, a quick glimpse into some top events to watch out for next week, and a list of some stories you may have missed amid all the market ructions.

Today's Market Minute

  • Trump has suspended the 25% tariffs he had imposed this week on most goods from Canada and Mexico, sowing more confusion in already whipsawed markets and fanning worries about inflation and growth.

  • Justin Trudeau says the U.S. tariff war is going to drag on for the foreseeable future. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says the Canadian premier is a 'numbskull'.

  • Trump has signed an executive order to establish a strategic bitcoin reserve, but the crypto world isn't wowed.

  • European leaders have agreed to hike their defence spending and to stand by Ukraine in a world upended by Trump's reversal of U.S. policies.

  • And finally, the market will get a look at the first monthly non-farm payrolls report under Trump's watch.

Wall St turns to jobs picture

On or off? Partial or universal? Higher or lower? Business and markets have no clear idea what U.S. import tariffs are going to be, a gnawing uncertainty about trade and prices that could potentially prove as corrosive as the tariffs themselves.

Attention now turns squarely to the labor market on Friday with the release of February's U.S. payrolls report.

Markets are already on edge following Thursday's news that planned U.S. layoffs soared last month to the highest point since the pandemic recession in 2020, even though weekly jobless claims fell unexpectedly. Meanwhile, private sector jobs in February rose only about half the amount forecast, based on the ADP report released earlier this week.

The Atlanta Federal Reserve's real time 'GDPNow' model is still tracking a contraction for the economy of 2.4% for the first quarter, though the forecast did improve slightly in recent days.

While chipmaker Broadcom's post-bell surge on its latest results helped steady U.S. stock futures early on Friday, Wall Street stocks have recorded another rough week.

Heavy losses on Thursday saw the S&P 500 clock its biggest daily loss of the year so far, as it hit its lowest point since before U.S. President Donald Trump's was re-elected on November 5. And for chart-watchers, the index fell below its closely-watched 200 day moving average.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq is now in what's known as correction territory, having recoiled more than 10% from its December record high. Bitcoin fell back again, with traders disappointed by Trump's latest plans for a crypto reserve.

Global shares fell broadly on Friday too, following Wall Street's lead amid U.S. trade and recession fears.

But even after the latest European Central Bank interest rate cut on Thursday, the euro continued its march higher against the ailing dollar , with European leaders agreeing on a 150 billion euro defense fund overnight to add to Germany's seismic fiscal stimulus plans announced earlier in the week.

The euro is now on course for its best week against the dollar in 16 years.

Today's key chart

Layoffs announced by U.S. employers jumped to levels not seen since the last two recessions amid mass federal government job cuts, canceled contracts and fears of trade wars. Global outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas said planned job cuts vaulted 245% month-over-month to 172,017 in February, the highest level since July 2020 when the economy was in the grips of the COVID-19 pandemic and the highest February total since the Great Recession 16 years ago.

Today's events to watch

  • U.S. February employment report, January consumer credit; Canada February employment report; Mexico February inflation

  • Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, Fed Board Governor Adriana Kugler, New York Fed President John Williams and Atlanta Fed boss Raphael Bostic all speak; European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde speaks; Bank of England policymaker Catherine Mann speaks

Top events to watch for next week

  • For an overview of what to watch out for next week, check out: Take Five: Change is in the air.

  • February's U.S. consumer price inflation report next week comes too early to capture the impact of the latest tariff hikes. But the extent to which inflation remains 'hot' and may get aggravated further by the tariffs will be a key debate. Inflation proved politically toxic for the previous administration of Joe Biden, and Trump's early approval rating on cost of living issues is already poor.

  • Europe's finance ministers meet next week to discuss the momentous turn in the continent's security and fiscal picture.

Stories away from major macro developments that you might have missed this week:

  • How ECB dodged a payment disaster in 10 hours of tech meltdown

  • US mulls plan to disrupt Iran's oil by halting vessels at sea

  • Trump-Zelenskiy clash exposes divisions in Europe's rising far right

  • Shipping firms pull back from Hong Kong to skirt U.S.-China risks

  • Republican officials rethink BlackRock bans after Panama port deal

data.title: SpaceX Again Loses Spacecraft During Latest Starship Test Flight — WSJ
data.description:
By Micah Maidenberg

SpaceX launched another Starship rocket Thursday but lost the vehicle's spacecraft not long after liftoff, the second such setback in a row for Elon Musk's company.

Minutes after launch, the rocket began experiencing problems while it ascended and it later exploded. A company commentator said on a launch livestream that the vehicle had gone into a spin and that measures were being put in place to guard against potential debris.

"With a test like this, success comes from what we learn, and today's flight will help us improve Starship's reliability," SpaceX said in a post on X. The company pledged to investigate the situation, in coordination with the Federal Aviation Administration, and "implement corrective actions to make improvements on future Starship flight tests."

The FAA, which regulates private space launches, said it managed air traffic, including halting some flights, to ensure planes avoided areas where spacecraft debris fell.

Flights were diverted in parts of the Caribbean, the aviation tracker Flightradar24 said in a post on X. Airline departures were delayed from Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood and Miami international airports, according to the FAA.

Before the explosion, SpaceX successfully recaptured the rocket's booster, which navigated to rest between what the company calls "chopstick" arms on the launchpad. It was the third time SpaceX was able to guide down the booster, critical to the company's longer-range ambitions to launch the rockets regularly.

The roughly 400-foot-tall experimental rocket blasted off from a SpaceX complex east of Brownsville, Texas, around 6:30 p.m. ET. Starship has two stages: a booster and a spacecraft that is stacked on top of the booster for liftoff. The spacecraft separates from the booster shortly after launch.

In January, SpaceX suffered a similar setback when a Starship spacecraft blew up not long after liftoff, prompting the FAA to implement airspace restrictions to guard against planes' being hit by debris.

No aircraft was struck, but some debris landed in Turks and Caicos, generating complaints from island residents who reported finding rocket components washing ashore.

The FAA gave SpaceX permission to conduct Thursday's flight while the company continues investigating the January explosion.

Thursday's launch marked the eighth time SpaceX launched the Starship rocket since the company started testing the vehicle in flight in April 2023.

SpaceX has said that during the January launch, hardware on the spacecraft's propulsion system faced more-intense stress than expected and fires broke out in a section of the vehicle.

The government of Turks and Caicos said recently that cleanup is continuing, and asked residents to report debris locations.

Because of the problem with Thursday's flight, SpaceX wasn't able to attempt other tests and objectives for the mission, such as deploying four devices designed to simulate satellites the company plans to use in its Starlink satellite-internet network. After flying through space, the vehicle was intended to land in the Indian Ocean a little over an hour after launch.

Write to Micah Maidenberg at micah.maidenberg@wsj.com

data.title: 908 Devices Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
908 Devices Inc., a leading provider of innovative analytical devices for chemical analysis, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing the company's financial and operational performance for the fiscal year. The report highlights significant growth in revenue, strategic initiatives to enhance product offerings, and the challenges faced in a dynamic market environment.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $59.6 million, reflecting a 19% increase from the previous year, driven by product and service revenue growth.

Gross Profit: $29.9 million, representing a gross profit margin of 50%, slightly down from the previous year due to increased production costs.

Loss from Operations: $(76.7) million, significantly impacted by a $40.7 million goodwill impairment charge.

Net Loss: $(72.2) million, compared to a net loss of $(36.4) million in the prior year, primarily due to increased operating expenses and goodwill impairment.

Net Loss Per Share: $(2.12), compared to $(1.13) in the previous year, reflecting the increased net loss.

Business Highlights

Product Portfolio: The company offers a suite of handheld and desktop devices for point-of-need chemical analysis, including MX908, ThreatID, ProtectIR, XplorIR, Rebel, ZipChip Interface, Maverick, and Maven. These devices leverage mass spectrometry, optical spectroscopy, microfluidics, and machine learning technologies to provide quick, actionable answers in various markets such as forensics, bioprocessing, and life sciences research.

Sales Units: Since the launch of the first device, over 3,500 handheld and desktop devices have been sold to more than 900 customers in 63 countries, including all top 20 pharmaceutical companies by revenue.

New Product Launches: In 2023, the company introduced the Maverick, an optical in-line analyzer for real-time monitoring and control of bioprocess parameters. The acquisition of RedWave in April 2024 added FTIR technology to the product portfolio, enhancing capabilities in chemical identification.

Geographical Performance: The company has expanded its device installations to 63 countries, with a focus on growing its presence in regions with a strong life sciences presence, such as Europe, China, Japan, India, and South Korea.

New Production Launches: In 2024, the company announced plans to move handheld manufacturing to Danbury, CT, and desktop manufacturing to Morrisville, NC, to reduce costs and improve operational efficiency.

Future Outlook: The company plans to continue expanding its commercial channels and deepen its footprint in the bioprocessing market. It aims to leverage its technology platform to penetrate new and adjacent applications and markets.

Recurring Revenue: Recurring revenue, including consumables and services, accounted for 39% of total revenue in 2024, up from 33% in 2023. This growth is expected to continue as the installed base of devices expands.

Operational Efficiency: In November 2024, the company implemented an organizational restructuring to enhance operational efficiencies, including a workforce reduction and the transition of manufacturing operations to lower-cost locations.

Customer Segments: The primary users of handheld devices include law enforcement, military, and first responders, while desktop devices are primarily used by pharmaceutical, biotechnology, and academic markets.

Technology Platform: The company's technology platform integrates microscale Mass Spec, optical spectroscopy, and microfluidics to provide high-fidelity analysis at the point-of-need, supported by machine learning for actionable insights.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: The company acquired RedWave Technology to expand its portfolio of handheld chemical analysis devices, enhancing its forensic workflows and desktop portfolio for pharma Process Analytical Technology and industrial Quality Control applications. Additionally, the company announced an organizational restructuring to improve operational efficiencies, including a workforce reduction and transitioning manufacturing operations to North Carolina and Connecticut.

Capital Management: The company entered into a Default Waiver and First Amendment to its Loan and Security Agreement, reducing its revolving line of credit to $10 million and adjusting financial covenants. The company also issued 1,497,171 unregistered shares as part of the RedWave acquisition and repaid $15 million outstanding under its previous revolving credit facility. Cash and cash equivalents were reported at $44 million, with marketable securities at $25.6 million as of December 31, 2024.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued operating losses as it focuses on growing commercial sales and scaling operations. It plans to finance operations through equity offerings, debt financings, and strategic alliances, while closely monitoring macroeconomic factors such as inflation and interest rates. The company expects its existing cash and cash equivalents to fund operations for at least the next 12 months, but acknowledges potential needs for additional funding to support its strategic initiatives.

Challenges and Risks

Challenges and Risks: The company faces significant risks related to macroeconomic conditions, including uncertainties in global economic conditions, inflation, and geopolitical tensions such as the military conflict between Russia and Ukraine and hostilities in Israel and the Gaza Strip. These factors could impact customer spending patterns and adversely affect financial condition and operating results. Additionally, the company is exposed to risks from pandemics, such as COVID-19, which may disrupt operations and supply chains, leading to reduced sales and cash flows.

The company has a history of net losses and may not achieve profitability in the future. Operating results may fluctuate significantly due to dependence on large orders from U.S. government agencies, market volatility, and other factors. The company also faces challenges in managing growth, which could affect product quality and the ability to retain key personnel.

The company must innovate and adapt to rapid technological changes to remain competitive. Failure to develop new products or enhance existing ones could harm market acceptance and competitive position. The company also relies on a limited number of suppliers for key components, posing risks of supply chain disruptions.

Management acknowledges the challenges of maintaining profitability and managing growth. The company plans to invest in sales, marketing, and research and development to drive revenue growth. However, there is a risk that these investments may not yield the expected returns, impacting financial performance.

The company is exposed to market risks, including foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, which could adversely affect financial results. Additionally, the company faces risks related to its international operations, such as geopolitical conditions, compliance with foreign regulations, and potential disruptions from natural disasters or public health crises.

Overall, the company is implementing strategies to address these risks, including diversifying sales channels, enhancing product offerings, and expanding international operations. However, the effectiveness of these strategies remains uncertain, and the company must navigate a complex and evolving risk landscape.
